## [00:00 - 03:06]

Okay, so one of the important concepts, or actually one of the most important words, and this is a word some people don't like so much, it's a four letter word, and that word is math. And math is pretty important in the information marketing business. And it's very simple math, it's not real complicated stuff, it's basically, you know, multiplication and division, and maybe some addition and subtraction in there as well. But it's not very complicated stuff, so stick with me, this is exciting stuff and you have to understand this. You know, all business, probably all business, but certainly all internet business, I think comes down to basically an arbitrage play. An arbitrage is where you buy something in one market for one price and you sell it in another market for another price. Hopefully you're selling it for more than you're buying it for. So in your internet business, you're pulling in traffic and you're getting that traffic through something. You're spending money or effort to get that traffic. And then you're trying to extract more value out of that traffic than you spend for the resources to get that traffic. So, you know, basically, when you're, you know, I talked about perceived value and selling and how you want to be selling at higher prices, and you'll see how that comes into play a lot with this math. But, you know, I recently had this experience where someone asked me, this is a very, very successful person. It was actually in a mastermind, in a high-end mastermind group that I'm in. An extremely successful person who is extremely good at math is business of millions and millions of dollars of sales. He works with some very high-powered people. But he asked, like, what's a reasonable refund rate for my product? And I said, you know, there is nothing, there's nothing's reasonable, it's just math. And so this is an example of where math comes into play. Say you have a product and it has a 10% refund rate, okay? Say one person or one product, one sales process has a 10% refund rate, and someone else has a 50% refund rate. Now you would think, wow, man, 50%, that's awful, right? That's five times worse than 10%. That's half the stuff you sell is coming back. But is it really awful? Well, that's just one piece of data, and you cannot make a judgment based on that one piece of data. Like, say for example, these are two different products, and this product is selling for $100, and this product is selling for $1,000, and you're selling 10 each, okay? So 10 units, you know, so that's 100 times 10, you've got $1,000 in sales,

## [03:06 - 04:51]

minus the 10% refunds, you'd end up with $900 net, okay? Now the same 10 sales over here, and you've got $10,000 in sales, 50% refund, you give 50% of the money back, and you end up with $5,000. Someone comes to your website, and you're, say you have a website, and you know, you're, you just kill it with your sales process, and you're converting 5% of your visitors into buyers. If they're selling the $100 product, and if they're selling a $10,000 product, and they each get 100 visitors, they've made $10,000 sale, and these people have sold 5 sales at 100. So again, the conversion rates are all over the board.
